communicated (anatithemi) laid or set before them The present tense suggests that the gospel he preached then is the gospel he still preaches now.

Oxen and garlands (taurouv kai stemmata). Probably garlands to put on the oxen before they were slain. It was common to sacrifice bullocks to Jupiter and Mercury. (Robertson's NT Word Pictures) - garlands are put upon victims

And there came thither certain Jews. Not satisfied with having expelled them from Antioch and Iconium, they still pursued them. (Barnes NT Notes)

"…in Acts, fourteenth chapter and twenty-third verse, we read: 'And when they had ordained them elders in every church, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they believed.' 'And when they were come to Jerusalem, they were received of the church, and of the apostles and elders. * * * And the apostles and elders came together.' (Acts 15:4-6.) 'And as they went…

When they had spoken the word in Perga (lalhsantev en pergh ton logon). Now they stopped and preached in Perga which they had apparently not done before (see #13:13). After leaving Antioch they passed on through Pisidia, as if Antioch was not strictly in Pisidia (see on #13:14) and into Pamphylia. They crossed from Perga to Attaleia, the port of Perga, sixteen miles down the Cestus, and capital…

When they had spoken the word in Perga (lalhsantev en pergh ton logon). Now they stopped and preached in Perga which they had apparently not done before (see #13:13). After leaving Antioch they passed on through Pisidia, as if Antioch was not strictly in Pisidia (see on #13:14) and into Pamphylia. They crossed from Perga to Attaleia, the port of Perga, sixteen miles down the Cestus, and capital…

They came to Antioch in Pisidia. Pisidia was a province of Asia Minor, and was situated north of Pamphylia. Antioch was not in Pisidia, but within the limits of Phrygia; but it belonged to Pisidia, and was called Antioch of Pisidia to distinguish it from Antioch in Syria. (Barnes NT Notes)

When they had spoken the word in Perga (lalhsantev en pergh ton logon). Now they stopped and preached in Perga which they had apparently not done before (see #13:13). After leaving Antioch they passed on through Pisidia, as if Antioch was not strictly in Pisidia (see on #13:14) and into Pamphylia. They crossed from Perga to Attaleia, the port of Perga, sixteen miles down the Cestus, and capital…

They came to Antioch in Pisidia. Pisidia was a province of Asia Minor, and was situated north of Pamphylia. Antioch was not in Pisidia, but within the limits of Phrygia; but it belonged to Pisidia, and was called Antioch of Pisidia to distinguish it from Antioch in Syria. (Barnes NT Notes)
Pamphylia. The country on the coast of Asia Minor between Lycia and Cilicia; Side is a sea-port of Pamphylia.
